statusLine: EU fuel stocks are under strain in March 2026, with 8 of 27 member states showing at least one fuel type below the 90-day mandatory minimum across all three product categories, and jet fuel emerging as a near-universal vulnerability.
fullAnalysis:
As of March 2026, the EU's aggregate oil stock position sits at a 'watch' level, with average reserves of 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel. While the petrol average remains nominally above the 90-day mandatory minimum, both diesel and jet fuel averages fall below it — a meaningful deterioration that reflects the ongoing strain from Middle East supply disruptions and the partial drawdown of strategic reserves that began in late February. Brent crude has risen 9.1% since the pre-escalation close of $72.48/barrel on 23 February, now trading at $79.07/barrel, adding cost pressure on top of supply tightness.

The most acute concerns are concentrated in Central and Southern Europe. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, Portugal, and Italy each carry a critical rating across all three fuel types, with some figures well below the mandatory threshold. Romania's diesel stands at just 30.2 days — less than a third of the required minimum — while Portugal's jet fuel is at 21.2 days and Poland's at 22.5 days. These are not marginal shortfalls; they represent structural gaps that would require significant import acceleration or reserve releases to close in the near term. Spain, France, Hungary, and Czechia also show critical readings in at least two of the three fuel categories.

Jet fuel is the single most widespread vulnerability in this dataset. Of the 27 member states, 20 carry a critical or warning rating for jet fuel stocks, compared to 15 for petrol and 14 for diesel. Only Ireland, Estonia, Slovenia, Croatia, and Sweden hold jet fuel stocks comfortably above the mandatory minimum. This is a notable asymmetry: several countries with adequate petrol and diesel buffers — including Finland (197.7 days diesel), the Netherlands (207.5 days diesel), and Latvia (250.6 days petrol) — still register critical jet fuel positions. This suggests the aviation supply chain is facing distinct procurement or logistics constraints separate from the broader petroleum market.

At the other end of the spectrum, the Baltic states, Ireland, and the Nordic countries generally present a stronger reserve picture, particularly for petrol and diesel. Estonia (208.2 days petrol, 124.3 days diesel) and Latvia (250.6 days petrol, 104.8 days diesel) maintain substantial buffers, likely reflecting strategic stockpiling policies and lower consumption bases. These countries provide limited systemic relief for the bloc as a whole, however, given the geographic concentration of shortfalls in high-consumption markets. Fuel price data as of 8 June shows the EU petrol average at €1.786/L and diesel at €1.804/L, with notable divergence between higher-tax markets such as Denmark (€2.392/L petrol) and lower-price markets such as Malta (€1.340/L) and Poland (€1.399/L) — differences that reflect taxation structures more than supply conditions, but which will bear watching if wholesale prices continue rising.
keyPoints: Both the EU diesel average (88.1 days) and jet fuel average (87.0 days) fall below the 90-day mandatory minimum as of March 2026, with only petrol (95.6 days) marginally above the threshold.

6 of 27 member states — Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, Portugal, and Italy — are rated critical across all three fuel types simultaneously, with Romania's diesel at just 30.2 days and Portugal's jet fuel at 21.2 days.

Jet fuel is the most pervasive weakness: 20 of 27 member states carry a critical or warning rating, including countries with otherwise strong petrol and diesel positions such as Finland, Latvia, and the Netherlands.

Brent crude has risen 9.1% to $79.07/barrel since the late-February supply escalation, compressing the economic incentive for commercial restocking at a time when reserve levels are already under pressure.

A small group of member states — notably Estonia, Latvia, Ireland, and Finland — maintain robust petrol and diesel buffers well above the mandatory minimum, though their geographic position limits their ability to relieve pressure in the most affected Central and Southern European markets.
